FOODHUB ASSIGNMENT¶

BY PRATEEK K SINGH

START¶

In [1]:
#Installing packages
#pip install seaborn -U -q
#pip install nltk -U -q
In [2]:
# Importing packages for data exploration,number crunching ,visualization.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import nltk as nl
In [3]:
#Reading Data
df=pd.read_csv("foodhub_order.csv")

Quick Insights-1¶

  1. There is no null value in any column/row.
  2. There is no duplicate values in the data.

df.dtypes

In [10]:
#Change "Not given" rating to "0" & column data type to integer for better visulaization and understanding.
df['rating']=df['rating'].replace('Not given',0)
df['rating']=df['rating'].astype(int)
In [11]:
#Change restaurant_name,cuisine_type,day_of_the_week columns data type to "string". 
df[['restaurant_name','cuisine_type','day_of_the_week']]=df[['restaurant_name','cuisine_type','day_of_the_week']].astype('string')
df['order_id']=df['order_id'].astype(str)
df['customer_id']=df['customer_id'].astype(str)

In [14]:
# Count orders per customer 
customer_order_count = (
    df.groupby('customer_id').agg(count=('customer_id', 'size'),restaurants=('restaurant_name', list)).reset_index()
)
# Keep only customers with more than 1 order and sort descending
repeat_customers = customer_order_count[customer_order_count['count'] > 1]\
                    .sort_values(by='count', ascending=False)\
                    .reset_index(drop=True)

repeat_customers

order_count = (
    df.groupby('order_id')
      .size()
      .reset_index(name='count')
).sort_values(by='count', ascending=False)\
                    .reset_index(drop=True)

Delivery Time¶

In [21]:
sns.histplot(data=df,x='delivery_time')
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data=df,x='delivery_time')
plt.show()

Quick Insights – 3¶

  1. A large proportion of orders fall between 10 and 20, showing this is the most common spending range.
  2. A small number of high-value orders (30+) exist, which may represent: bulk orders, premium restaurants, group orders.
  3. Rating suggests there are highly satified customers.
  4. Preparation & delivery time indicates fairly controlled/consistent cooking and delivery.

Univariate Analysis(Categorical)¶

In [22]:
plt.figure(figsize=(5, 10))
g=sns.catplot(
    data=df,
    y="restaurant_name",
    kind="count",
    height=20,
    aspect=0.5
)
ax = g.ax
for p in ax.patches:
    width = p.get_width()  # length of the bar
    ax.text(
        width + 0.1,        # x-position: slightly after the end of the bar
        p.get_y() + p.get_height()/2,  # y-position: center of the bar
        int(width),         # display count as integer
        ha='left',          # horizontal alignment
        va='center'         # vertical alignment
    )
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Quick Insights – 4¶

  1. Shake Shack, Blue Ribbon Sushi, The Meatball Shop are top 3 ordered restaurants respectively.
  2. American, Japanese and Italian are most ordered cuisines.
  3. Weekend orders are approx 2x more than Weekday orders.

Bivariate Analysis(Numerical)¶

In [25]:
#Checking for correlation betweeen variables.
num_var = df.dtypes[df.dtypes != 'object'].index
corr = df[num_var].corr(numeric_only = True)
# plot the heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, vmin=-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [26]:
sns.pairplot(data=df[num_var], diag_kind="kde")
plt.show()

There does not seem to be a strong relationship between any variable.¶

Food Preparation Time¶

In [27]:
#Restaurants with more than 10 orders were selected for further analysis to minimize noise.
valid_restaurants = (df["restaurant_name"].value_counts().loc[lambda x: x > 10].index)
df_filtered = df[df["restaurant_name"].isin(valid_restaurants)]
plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
sns.boxplot(data=df_filtered,x="food_preparation_time",y="restaurant_name")
plt.xlabel("Food Preparation Time (minutes)")
plt.ylabel("Restaurant Name")
plt.title("Restaurant vs Food Preparation Time (Count > 10)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
valid_restaurants = (df["restaurant_name"].value_counts().loc[lambda x: x > 10].index)
df_filtered = df[df["restaurant_name"].isin(valid_restaurants)]
sns.boxplot(data=df_filtered,x="delivery_time",y="restaurant_name")
plt.xlabel("Food Delivery Time (minutes)")
plt.ylabel("Restaurant Name")
plt.title("Restaurant vs Food Delivery Time (Count > 10)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [61]:
#Restaurants offering the fastest end-to-end home delivery (order placement to delivery). 
restaurant_time = (
    df_filtered.groupby('restaurant_name')
      .agg(
          mean_prep_time=('food_preparation_time', 'mean'),
          mean_delivery_time=('delivery_time', 'mean')
      )
)

restaurant_time['total_time'] = (
    restaurant_time['mean_prep_time'] +
    restaurant_time['mean_delivery_time']
)

restaurant_time = restaurant_time.sort_values('total_time', ascending=True)
plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
sns.catplot(x='restaurant_name',y='total_time',data=restaurant_time,  kind="bar" )
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('Restaurant')
plt.ylabel('Avg Prep + Delivery Time (mins)')
plt.title('Total Average Time by Restaurant')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

<Figure size 700x700 with 0 Axes>
In [42]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
valid_restaurants = (df["restaurant_name"].value_counts().loc[lambda x: x > 10].index)
df_filtered = df[df["restaurant_name"].isin(valid_restaurants)]
sns.boxplot(data=df_filtered,x="rating",y="restaurant_name")
plt.xlabel("Rating")
plt.ylabel("Restaurant Name")
plt.title("Restaurant vs Rating (Count > 10)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
sns.boxplot(data=df_filtered,x="cost_of_the_order",y="restaurant_name")
plt.xlabel("Cost of Order")
plt.ylabel("Restaurant Name")
plt.title("Restaurant vs Cost of Order (Count > 10)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

In [71]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
sns.boxplot(data=df,x="cost_of_the_order",y="cuisine_type")
plt.xlabel("Cost of Order")

In [47]:
cuisine_time = (
    df.groupby('cuisine_type')
      .agg(
          mean_prep_time=('food_preparation_time', 'mean'),
          mean_delivery_time=('delivery_time', 'mean')
      )
)

cuisine_time['total_time'] = (
    cuisine_time['mean_prep_time'] +
    cuisine_time['mean_delivery_time']
)

cuisine_time = cuisine_time.sort_values('total_time', ascending=True)
plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
plt.bar(cuisine_time.index, cuisine_time['total_time'])
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('Cuisine')
plt.ylabel('Avg Prep + Delivery Time (mins)')
plt.title('Total Average Time by Cuisine')
plt.show()

Quick Insights – 5¶

  1. The median prep time for most restaurants is ~26–29 minutes.
  2. Cafe Mogador,Burger Joint,Hill Country Fried Chicken are operationally efficient with smaller variability in preparation time.
  3. Korean & Vietnamese are cost friendly options for frequent ordering.
  4. Westville Hudson/Cafe Mogador or Korean/Spanish are fastest to order restaurants and Cuisines to home.

Multivariate Analysis¶

Trends and patterns for Weekday and Weekend.

Quick Insights – 6¶

  1. Delivery time is higher on weekdays for all restaurants, cuisine type and cost of order.

Recommendations¶

Restaurant¶

  1. Several restaurants show large spreads in food prep/delivery time and should be examined for Kitchen bottlenecks, Peak-hour staffing, Menu complexity for reducing unpredictable customer experience.
  2. Restaurants(Cafe Mogador/ilili) are fastest to order with lower median and consistent food prep are ideal for: Featured listings, Subscription/free-delivery campaigns.

Cuisine¶

  1. Korean/Vietnamese are fastest to prepare/ affordable options and can be promoted as Quick delivery items.
  2. American,Japanese,Italian,Chinese though highly ordered and cost similar, restaurants can batch-prep or pre-stage ingredients, leading to shorter and more consistent preparation times.

Weekend vs Weekday Strategy¶

  1. Weekday > Weekend delivery times. To address higher weekday delivery times, delivery partners should be incentivized to increase weekday availability through targeted incentives and shift-based rewards.

END¶

In [ ]:
